You drank the bitter tonic in one go. They watch your face for a reaction and find none. Iron constitution. The tonic recipe: the healer sells it at three times the cost of the ingredients. The margin pays a monthly fee to the council for her license to practice.

You bore the stinging nettles along the shortcut without flinching. They watch you brush off the welts. Most people turn back at the first sting. The shortcut leads past the council's private storehouse. They grow the nettles on purpose to discourage foot traffic.
RESISTANCE: You shake off the drowsiness. Their eyes widen slightly. That tea usually puts people at ease: too much ease. Since you seem clear-headed, I will tell you plainly. The calming herbs the healer sells work a little too well. People stop asking questions after a few cups.

The dread fades and you hold your ground. They shift uncomfortably. Most people leave when that feeling hits. Since you did not: the trinkets the peddler sells are not just trinkets. People who buy them stop caring about the things they used to worry about.

You resist the urge to agree and they notice the resistance in your eyes. Fine. The elder's speeches at the assembly: have you noticed nobody ever disagrees? It is not respect. He has been burning a particular incense during meetings for years.
The pressure lifts and your thoughts clear. They drop the pretense. That charm I keep on the shelf: it was given to me by a woman who said it would keep trouble away. What it keeps away is questions.

You hold steady against the pull to leave. They sigh and close the door. Most people are already gone by now. The amulet the merchant sells for protection: it does not protect. It makes people forget what they were worried about.
The fog clears from your mind and you stare at them evenly. Impressive. The pendant I wear: it was a gift from the herbalist. She said it would ease conversation. What it eases is resistance.

You blink through the haze and your focus returns. They set down the cup. The tea blend is her recipe, not mine. She gives it to half the town. People drink it and feel content. Even when they should not.

The compulsion to trust them passes, and they can see it in your face. Well. Since that did not work: the council meetings always start with a particular herbal infusion. Nobody votes against the agenda. Nobody ever questions why.

You push through the pleasant numbness and they lose their composure. That was supposed to settle things. Fine. The candles in the council hall are not ordinary beeswax. There is something mixed in. The air during meetings makes everyone agreeable.

The charm's influence slides off you and they look genuinely surprised. You are stronger-willed than most. The old woman who sells blessings at the market: her blessings work. But they work by dulling the edges of concern, not by solving problems.
You snap out of the daze and they back away from the table. That stone on the counter: I was told it would keep visitors from asking too many questions. It has worked on everyone until now.

The strange calm evaporates and you feel yourself again. They notice the clarity return to your eyes. The wind chimes the council installed last year: they are not decorative. On certain nights they produce a tone that makes people sleep deeper than they should.
You shake off the lethargy. They look at you differently now. The smoke from the elder's hearth: it drifts across the square in the evenings. People who breathe it feel calm. Unnaturally calm.

The urge to leave dissolves and you plant your feet. They stop smiling. That fragrance in the room: it is not perfume. The herbalist blends it to soothe visitors. I use it because some conversations are easier when the other person does not push back.

You resist the impulse to agree and they set their hands flat on the table. You are difficult. That is a compliment. The drinking water from the council's private well tastes slightly sweet. It should not. Something is being added.

The mental fog recedes and you see them clearly for the first time. They seem smaller without the effect. The reason nobody in town complains about the council: it is not satisfaction. It is the tea. Everyone drinks it. Everyone is content. Nobody should be this content.

You break free of the pleasant sensation and they look almost frightened. I have never seen anyone resist that. The sachets the healer distributes for headaches: they cure the headache and replace it with compliance. I have watched it happen.

The buzzing in your ears stops and sound returns to normal. They stop talking and study you. The bell at the top of the hour: it is not just for timekeeping. People who hear it regularly become less inclined to question things. I noticed when I was away for a month.

You resist the pull of the pendant and their face goes pale. Nobody does that. The jeweler who made it said it encourages trust. What it really encourages is obedience. Half the shopkeepers in town wear one.

The warmth drains away and cold clarity takes its place. They exhale slowly. I have been using that herb for so long I forgot what honest conversation feels like. The truth is, half the town is sleepwalking through their lives and the herbalist is the reason.

You push past the compulsion and their mask slips. The scent in the bakery is not just bread. The baker uses an additive the herbalist provides. Regular customers are loyal not because of the quality: because of the effect.

The weight on your mind lifts and you think clearly again. They watch you with something between fear and respect. The reason the council faces no opposition is not political skill. It is the water. The aqueduct passes through the herbalist's garden.

You reject the calm that tried to settle over you. They look away. Fine. The festival last year: the incense they burned during the opening ceremony was not ceremonial. For three days, nobody argued about anything. That is not natural.

The pressure behind your eyes subsides. They wring their hands. I was told the charm would protect my business by keeping customers relaxed. It does. But relaxed people do not notice when they are overcharged.

You shake off the influence and stand taller. They seem to shrink. The perfume the elder's wife wears at public events: people around her become agreeable. Votes go her husband's way. It has happened at every assembly for two years.

The urge to leave fades and you remain. They look around as if expecting someone else to intervene. The dried flowers in the doorways around town: they are from the same plant. The herbalist distributes them. She says they ward off illness. They ward off suspicion.

You resist the soothing tone and their voice returns to normal. They sag. I have been using that technique for so long I do it without thinking. The trader taught it to me. He uses the same trick everywhere he goes. People buy more when they are pliable.

The dreamy quality leaves your thoughts and hard edges return. They set the cup down between you. The tea is the key. Whoever controls the tea supply controls the mood of the town. The herbalist knows this. The council knows this. Nobody else does.

You break free of the mental fog and they stop mid-sentence. I have never had to actually explain myself before. The wards on the doorframe: they are not for protection. They are for persuasion. Anyone who crosses the threshold becomes more agreeable.

The haze clears and you feel sharp again. They lean back and fold their arms. Fine. You earned this. The entire town is under a gentle influence, and it comes from the water, the tea, and the incense. The herbalist maintains all three, and the council pays her well to do it.

You push through the disorientation and stand firm. They look almost relieved. I was hoping someone would resist eventually. The charm the council distributes at festivals: it makes people feel generous. Generous enough to overpay at every stall. The extra goes to the organizers.

The heaviness leaves your limbs and you feel light again. They put the stone back in its box. That was found in the old mine. Whoever holds it feels calm and trusting. The elder keeps one on his desk during every meeting.

You blink away the haze and they see the sharpness return to your eyes. Good. The sachets people hang in their doorways: the herbalist says they are for luck. They are not. They make people sleep deeply and dream peacefully. Too peacefully to wake when things happen at night.

The pleasant feeling evaporates and you recognize it for what it was. They fold their hands. The reason the tavern feels so welcoming is not the atmosphere. The incense the owner burns contains something the herbalist provides. People stay longer, drink more, and remember less.

You shake off the influence and they sit down heavily. I have never met anyone who could do that. The water from the council well: it passes through a filter the herbalist installed. People who drink it regularly stop asking difficult questions. It has been this way for years.

The fog lifts and your mind sharpens. They put away the amulet. Fine. The jewelry the peddler sells at the market: the stones are treated with something. People who wear them feel content. Complaints to the council dropped by half the season after he started selling them.

You resist the urge to nod and smile, and they see the defiance in your expression. The incense at the temple: the priest burns what the herbalist provides. The congregation is devout, but their devotion has a chemical component nobody examines.

The mental weight lifts and you think clearly. They remove the pendant from the table. That thing: the merchant who sold it said it promotes harmony. What it promotes is compliance. Three shops on this street have one. Their customers never haggle.

You push past the soothing sensation and they drop their smile. The dried flowers the council hands out at the spring planting: everyone puts them in their homes. People with the flowers report fewer worries. People without them report the same number of problems as always. Draw your own conclusion.

The compulsion breaks and your own thoughts flood back in. They look at you with grudging respect. The tea house on the corner: it is the most popular gathering place in town, and the most tranquil. But tranquility should not come from a cup. Something in the blend smooths every edge and quiets every objection.
